3 min read
Just now

--

As Laravel applications grow, managing access control, authentication, logging, and request filtering directly inside controllers can quickly become messy.
Fortunately, Laravel provides a powerful feature called Middleware that helps developers handle these concerns in a clean and organized way.
Middleware acts as a bridge between an incoming HTTP request and your application’s business logic. By mastering middleware, you can build more secure, maintainable, and scalable Laravel applications.
In this article, you’ll learn what middleware is, how it works, and how to use it effectively in real-world projects.

What Is Middleware?
Middleware is a mechanism for filtering HTTP requests entering your application.
Before a request reaches a controller, Laravel allows middleware to inspect, modify, allow, or reject the request.
Think of middleware as a checkpoint that every request must pass through before accessing specific parts of your application.
Common use cases include:

• Authentication

• Authorization

• Request logging

• API rate limiting

• Language localization

• Maintenance mode checks

How Middleware Works
A request enters your Laravel application.
Before reaching the controller, it passes through one or more middleware classes.
Each middleware can:

• Allow the request to continue

• Modify the request

• Return a custom response

• Block the request entirely
This architecture keeps controllers focused on business logic instead of repetitive checks.

Creating a Custom Middleware
Laravel makes it easy to create middleware.
Run the following command:
php artisan make:middleware EnsureUserIsAdminLaravel creates:
app/Http/Middleware/EnsureUserIsAdmin.php
Writing Middleware Logic
Inside the middleware:
public function handle($request, Closure $next)
{
if (!auth()->check() || !auth()->user()->is_admin) {
abort(403);
}return $next($request);
}
In this example:

• Unauthenticated users are blocked

• Non-admin users receive a 403 response

• Admin users are allowed to continue

Registering Middleware
To use your middleware, register it inside your application.
Example alias registration:
'admin' => App\Http\Middleware\EnsureUserIsAdmin::class,Once registered, it can be applied to routes.

Applying Middleware to Routes
Single route:
Route::get('/dashboard', function () {
return view('dashboard');
})->middleware('admin');Route group:
Route::middleware(['auth', 'admin'])
->group(function () {Route::get('/users', UserController::class);
Route::get('/settings', SettingsController::class);
});
This ensures all routes inside the group are protected.

Global Middleware
Some middleware should run on every request.
Examples include:

• Request trimming

• Maintenance mode checks

• Proxy handling
Laravel executes global middleware automatically before route-specific middleware.
This helps enforce application-wide rules consistently.

Middleware Parameters
Middleware can accept custom parameters.
Example:
public function handle(
$request,
Closure $next,
$role
) {
if (auth()->user()->role !== $role) {
abort(403);
}return $next($request);
}
Usage:
Route::middleware('role:admin')
->get('/admin', function () {
return 'Admin Area';
});This makes middleware highly reusable.

Logging Requests with Middleware
Middleware is an excellent place to track incoming requests.
Example:
public function handle($request, Closure $next)
{
Log::info('Request received', [
'url' => $request->fullUrl(),
'ip' => $request->ip(),
]);return $next($request);
}
This is useful for:

• Analytics

• Security monitoring

• Debugging

API Rate Limiting
One of the most common middleware use cases is rate limiting.
Example:
Route::middleware('throttle:60,1')
->group(function () {Route::get('/products', ProductController::class);
});
This limits users to 60 requests per minute.
Rate limiting helps protect APIs from abuse and excessive traffic.

Middleware Execution Order
Laravel processes middleware in the order they are assigned.
Example:
Route::middleware([
'auth',
'verified',
'admin'
])->group(function () {// Protected routes
});
Execution flow:

• Authentication check

• Email verification check

• Admin permission check
If any middleware fails, Laravel stops processing immediately.

Common Middleware Mistakes
A common mistake is placing business logic inside middleware.
Bad example:
public function handle($request, Closure $next)
{
Order::create([
'user_id' => auth()->id()
]);return $next($request);
}
Middleware should focus on request filtering, not business operations.
Keep business logic inside:

• Services

• Controllers

• Jobs

• Event Listeners

Best Practices
To get the most out of middleware:

• Keep middleware focused on a single responsibility

• Avoid complex database operations

• Use route groups when possible

• Reuse middleware through parameters

• Keep business logic out of middleware

• Use meaningful middleware names
Following these practices keeps applications clean and maintainable.

Final Thoughts
Middleware is one of Laravel’s most powerful architectural features.
It allows you to:

• Secure routes efficiently

• Filter requests cleanly

• Reuse access-control logic

• Improve application organization

• Keep controllers focused on business logic
As your Laravel applications grow, middleware becomes increasingly valuable for maintaining a clean and scalable codebase.
Developers who master middleware often write cleaner, more professional applications because they understand how to separate concerns effectively.
If you’re serious about Laravel development, middleware is a feature worth mastering early in your journey.
